---
title: "DOT Explainer"
draft: false
class: "402"

---

# .`dot` Files and Graphviz: An Overview and Resources

This course uses **DOT files (Graphviz)** as a simple, formal way to represent systems. DOT is not a drawing tool. It is a **text-based language** for describing the elements of a system and the connections between them.

.`dot` is used here because it encourages precision, transparency, and clarity of thinking about system structure.


> [!warning] Class Success Tip
> Remember, we are using systems maps as tools to understand how the US healthcare system works.  We will create systems maps using software.  `.dot` files are written in  a language that we will use to describe our healthcare system and create a map.

You can also follow along and look at my example `.dot` file on [heart disease](Heart-Disease-Example-Graphviz.qmd).  A downloadable file is available on Canvas.

# What a .`dot` File Is

A .`dot` file is a plain-text file that describes a graph.  A system map is a graph.

In a .`dot` file:

- **Elements** of a system are written as _nodes_
- **Connections** between elements are written as _edges_
- Direction matters: arrows encode influence or causation

A simple .`dot` file might look like this:

```
digraph {
  Population -> Resource_Use
  Resource_Use -> Environmental_Impact
}
```

In plain language, this .`dot` file is making the following claims:

- There is an element called **Population**.
- There is an element called **Resource Use**.
- There is an element called **Environmental Impact**.
- Changes in **Population** influence **Resource Use**.
- Changes in **Resource Use** influence **Environmental Impact**.

The arrows indicate the _direction_ of influence. This model does not claim that environmental impact directly affects population, nor does it claim that resource use affects population in return. Those connections are absent, and that absence is itself a modeling choice.

This file describes a system structure. How it looks visually depends on how it is rendered.


> [!important] Using Text Editors (Required)
> For this assignment, you will use a **plain text file** to describe your system map in Graphviz (.`dot`) format. A plain text file contains only words and symbols—no hidden formatting, fonts, or layout instructions. Text editors (such as Notepad, TextEdit in plain-text mode, or VS Code) are designed for this kind of file. We use plain text because it is transparent, stable, and precise: what you type is exactly what the system reads. This keeps the focus on causal structure and logic rather than visual design or software features. You are not being evaluated on technical skill—only on whether your system relationships are clearly and correctly specified.
> 
> Do not use Word or Google Docs for working on these files. Use only a text editor.  Notepad is the free text editor that comes on Windows.  Text Edit is the free text editor that comes on an Apple computer. 

# Why This Course Uses .`dot` files

.`dot` files are used because they: 

- Makes assumptions explicit
- Forces clarity about direction and influence
- Separates system structure from visual layout
- Supports feedback loops naturally
- Encourages thinking in terms of **elements and connections** (after Donella Meadows)
- Keeps you from worrying about drawing systems maps.

# What You Are Expected to Be Comfortable With

You do not need to memorize .`dot` syntax. You _do_ need to be able to work with simple examples.

You should aim to be comfortable with:

- Reading a .`dot` file and explaining what each node represents
- Following arrows and explaining what each connection claims
- Recognizing feedback loops as circular paths
- Creating a .`dot` file for your system map using a template.
- Making small edits to an existing .`dot` file
- Understanding that layout is separate from meaning

.`dot` is used to support systems thinking, not to test technical skill.

# .`dot` File Notation: Comments, Labels, and Formatting

.`dot` files support simple conventions that help make system structure clearer without changing meaning.  I*n addition to the examples below, open my example .dot file and examine it while you read the explanations below.*
## Comments

Comments allow you to leave notes in a .`dot` file that do not affect the graph.

- Single-line comments begin with `//`
- Multi-line comments are wrapped in `/* */`

Comments are useful for explaining modeling choices, assumptions, or uncertainties.

Example:

```
// This connection represents perceived influence, not measured causation
Population -> Resource_Use
```
## Labels for Polarity

Connections can be labeled to indicate whether an influence is **positive** or **negative**.

- A **positive (+)** polarity means that an increase in one element tends to increase the other (or a decrease tends to decrease it).
- A **negative (–)** polarity means that an increase in one element tends to decrease the other (or vice versa).

Example:

```
Population -> Resource_Use [label="+"]
Resource_Use -> Environmental_Impact [label="+"]
```

Labels describe the _direction of influence_, not its strength.

## Labels for Delays

Delays can be represented by labeling a connection or by inserting an intermediate element.

Example (labeled delay):

```
Resource_Use -> Environmental_Impact [label="+ (delay)"]
```

Example (explicit delay element):

```
Resource_Use -> Accumulated_Effects -> Environmental_Impact
```

Delays are important because they often explain why systems behave in unexpected or counterintuitive ways.

## Subgraphs and Clusters

You will be asked to show reinforcing and balancing loops in your project.  You can show those using subgraphs and clusters.

Here is an [example](https://graphs.grevian.org/example#example-6)

# Formatting and Readability

DOT allows basic formatting to improve readability:

- Labels can include spaces and explanatory text
- Nodes and edges can be grouped using clusters
- Visual attributes (such as color or shape) may be used sparingly for clarity

Formatting choices should support understanding, not decoration. Structure comes first.

# Tools for Working with .`dot`Files

You can work with .`dot`files in several ways:
## Graphviz 

This is the software that reads a .dot file and creates a graph from it. You can use it in two ways:
### Online renderers
These let you paste DOT code and see the output. This is the recommended approach.

[Graphviz Visual Editor](https://magjac.com/graphviz-visual-editor/)
[Graphviz Online](https://dreampuf.github.io/GraphvizOnline/) (This is a great first choice to use for class)
[Edotor](https://edotor.net/)
  
### Graphviz installed locally (command line rendering).  

If you are comfortable with installing software on your computer at the command line, feel free to install and use the Graphviz software on your computer.  Please reach out to me if you use this option.

## Text Editors

For this assignment, you will use a **plain text file** to describe your system map in Graphviz (.dot) format. A plain text file contains only words and symbols—no hidden formatting, fonts, or layout instructions. Text editors (such as Notepad, TextEdit in plain-text mode, or VS Code) are designed for this kind of file. We use plain text because it is transparent, stable, and precise: what you type is exactly what the system reads. This keeps the focus on causal structure and logic rather than visual design or software features. You are not being evaluated on technical skill—only on whether your system relationships are clearly and correctly specified.

> [!warning] Do Not Use Word or Google Docs
> Do not use Word or Google Docs for working on these files. Use only a text editor.  Notepad is the free text editor that comes on Windows.  Text Edit is the free text editor that comes on an Apple computer. 


Any workflow that lets you read, edit, and render DOT files is sufficient for this course.

# Recommended Resources

## Introductory Overviews

Graphviz official documentation: [What is Graphviz](https://graphviz.org/#what-is-graphviz)

@TheBuilder has a good [overview](https://youtu.be/YL260-A5r2U?si=aFiNNmHqAn1OkdY2) on YouTube.  (Stop at 9:35 in the video)    

[Graphviz (`.dot`) Examples](https://renenyffenegger.ch/notes/tools/Graphviz/examples/index) 

[Graphviz Pocket Reference](https://graphs.grevian.org/)

These explain the core ideas without assuming prior programming experience.

## Common Early Confusions

- **DOT files are not drawings.** They describe relationships, not positions.
- **Messy output usually means unclear structure**, not a formatting failure.
- **Feedback loops are not special symbols.** They emerge when connections form a cycle.
- **Clusters organize ideas but do not define system boundaries.**

If something looks confusing, the question to ask is usually: _What is this element connected to, and why?_
# Self-Check (Not Submitted)

Before using DOT in assignments, see if you can:

- Read a simple DOT file and explain what system it represents
    
- Describe what each arrow is claiming, in plain language
    
- Identify at least one feedback loop (or explain why none exist)
    
- Make a small change to a DOT file and predict how the structure will change
    

If you can do these things, you have enough DOT fluency for this course.

# The Main Idea Revisited

DOT is a language for making system structure explicit. It helps turn informal ideas about how things interact into clear, inspectable claims about **elements and connections**.

The goal is not to produce perfect diagrams, but to think clearly about systems.
